name: constructor-io-unguarded
description: A constructor performs external I/O (file writes, directory creation) or mutates shared error-name state as unconditional expression statements with no try/catch guard and no path condition, meaning any construction failure throws synchronously and leaves the object in a partially initialized, inconsistent state that callers cannot detect.
smt2Template:
(declare-const {{io_attempted}} Int)
(declare-const {{io_succeeded}} Int)
(declare-const {{object_fully_initialized}} Int)
(declare-const {{caller_notified_of_failure}} Int)
(declare-const {{guard_present}} Int)
; io is attempted unconditionally in constructor
(assert (= {{io_attempted}} 1))
; guard is absent
(assert (= {{guard_present}} 0))
; io can fail
(assert (or (= {{io_succeeded}} 0) (= {{io_succeeded}} 1)))
; if io fails and no guard, object is not fully initialized
(assert (=> (and (= {{io_succeeded}} 0) (= {{guard_present}} 0))
            (= {{object_fully_initialized}} 0)))
; caller is never notified — exception propagates from constructor
(assert (= {{caller_notified_of_failure}} 0))
; show satisfiable: io fails, object broken, caller blind
(assert (= {{io_succeeded}} 0))
(check-sat)

teachingExample.explanation: A PatientEventLogger class writes an audit directory and an initial log file unconditionally inside its constructor. On a read-only filesystem (e.g., after a storage fault), mkdirSync throws synchronously. The calling subsystem catches the constructor exception generically, discards the half-built logger object, and silently falls back to a null reference — causing all subsequent patient events to be dropped with no alert. Because the I/O is an unguarded expression statement inside the constructor with no try/catch, there is no way for the object to signal partial initialization or for the caller to distinguish a healthy logger from a broken one.
teachingExample.smt2:
(declare-const dir_create_attempted Int)
(declare-const dir_create_succeeded Int)
(declare-const file_write_attempted Int)
(declare-const file_write_succeeded Int)
(declare-const logger_initialized Int)
(declare-const events_recorded Int)
(declare-const guard_present Int)
(assert (= dir_create_attempted 1))
(assert (= guard_present 0))
; filesystem is read-only — both ops fail
(assert (= dir_create_succeeded 0))
(assert (= file_write_attempted 0))
(assert (= file_write_succeeded 0))
; without guard, initialization never completes
(assert (=> (and (= dir_create_succeeded 0) (= guard_present 0))
            (= logger_initialized 0)))
; no initialized logger means no events recorded
(assert (=> (= logger_initialized 0) (= events_recorded 0)))
(check-sat)
